summary: Juniper Street in Midtown is shut down for at least six weeks for emergency sewer repairs. Atlanta United picked up their first W of the season in style. A former USPS worker copped to stealing cash from mail at an Atlanta facility. And an Atlanta hairstylist just got an Oscar nomination for her work on 'Sinners.'

topStory.headline: Juniper Street Shut Down for Emergency Sewer Repairs, At Least 6 Weeks
topStory.summary: If you drive through Midtown, heads up. Atlanta's Department of Watershed Management closed Juniper Street for emergency sewer line repairs. The closure is expected to last at least six weeks, which means alternate routes through the area for a while.
topStory.body:
Atlanta's Department of Watershed Management has closed Juniper Street in Midtown for emergency sewer repairs, and the closure is expected to last at least six weeks. The repair work addresses a failing sewer line that city engineers flagged as a priority after recent inspections.

Drivers who normally use Juniper as a cut-through in Midtown will need to find alternate routes. The city is directing traffic to Piedmont Avenue and West Peachtree Street during the closure. MARTA remains unaffected, but street-level bus routes in the area may see minor rerouting.

The timing is rough for Midtown businesses along the corridor, especially restaurants and shops that rely on street-level visibility and parking. The city says they're working to minimize the disruption but there's no getting around a six-week dig.
topStory.imageAlt: Emergency sewer repair work on Juniper Street in Midtown Atlanta

stories.headline: Atlanta United Grabs First Win of the Season, 3-1 Over Philly
stories.summary: Mercedes-Benz Stadium was rocking Saturday as Atlanta United finally got their first win of the 2026 season, beating Philadelphia Union 3-1. The supporters section was in full voice all night.
stories.body:
Atlanta United supporters had something to actually celebrate Saturday night. The Five Stripes picked up their first win of the 2026 MLS season with a convincing 3-1 victory over Philadelphia Union at Mercedes-Benz Stadium.

After a rough start to the season that had fans getting restless, the team put together their best 90 minutes of the year. The energy at the Benz was noticeable from kickoff, and the team fed off it.

For a fanbase that's been spoiled since the 2018 MLS Cup, the early-season struggles were hard to swallow. Saturday's result won't erase the concerns, but it was exactly the kind of performance United needed to stop the bleeding and give the locker room some confidence heading into a tough stretch of fixtures.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta United fans celebrate at Mercedes-Benz Stadium

stories.headline: Atlanta Hairstylist Scores Oscar Nomination for 'Sinners'
stories.summary: 'Sinners' pulled a record 16 Oscar nominations, and one of them belongs to Atlanta's own Shunika Terry. She's nominated for her hair and makeup work on the film.
stories.body:
An Atlanta hairstylist who built her career making people look fabulous is heading to the Oscars. Shunika Terry earned an Academy Award nomination for her hair and makeup work on 'Sinners,' which received a record-breaking 16 nominations this year.

Terry has been a fixture in Atlanta's film and entertainment scene for years, working with clients across the city's booming production industry. Her nomination puts a spotlight on the deep bench of talent that makes Atlanta one of the biggest filmmaking hubs in the country.

The Oscars ceremony is coming up, and regardless of the outcome, Terry's nomination is a big deal for Atlanta's creative community. The city's film industry generates billions in economic impact, and stories like this remind everyone that the talent behind the camera is just as impressive as what ends up on screen.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta hairstylist Shunika Terry, Oscar nominee for Sinners

stories.headline: Mayor Throws Punches Instead of Signing Firefighter Contract
stories.summary: Atlanta Civic Circle reports the mayor is getting into public fights rather than finalizing the firefighter union contract. The union has been waiting on the deal for months.
stories.body:
Instead of putting pen to paper on the long-awaited firefighter union contract, Atlanta's mayor spent the week slinging mud at political opponents. Atlanta Civic Circle has the breakdown of how the negotiations have stalled while City Hall focuses on other battles.

The firefighters union has been at the table for months trying to lock down better pay and working conditions. Members say they're frustrated by the delays, especially as the city continues to struggle with recruitment and retention in the department.

The optics aren't great. While other cities are competing for experienced firefighters with better compensation packages, Atlanta's deal sits unsigned. The union says they're ready to finalize things whenever the mayor decides to show up.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta mayor and firefighter union contract dispute

stories.headline: Georgia House Passes Bill to Open Up Medical Cannabis Access
stories.summary: The Georgia House approved a bill aimed at modernizing the state's medical cannabis program and making it easier for patients to access. It now heads to the governor.
stories.body:
The Georgia House passed a bill this week that would make the state's medical cannabis program actually work for patients. The legislation, sponsored by Augusta Republican Rep. Mark Newton, aims to cut through the red tape that's kept Georgia's program from delivering on its promise.

Georgia legalized medical cannabis oil back in 2015 but the rollout has been painfully slow. Patients with qualifying conditions have struggled to access products through the limited number of licensed dispensaries. This bill would expand access points and streamline the licensing process.

The bill now heads to the governor's desk. Supporters say it's long overdue, pointing out that neighboring states have lapped Georgia in building functional medical cannabis programs. Whether the governor signs it remains to be seen, but the bipartisan House vote gives advocates reason to be optimistic.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia House chamber during medical cannabis vote

stories.headline: Former USPS Workers Plead Guilty to Stealing Cash from Atlanta Mail
stories.summary: Two former postal workers admitted in federal court to swiping cash and gift cards from mail at an Atlanta USPS facility. Not a great look for the post office.
stories.body:
Two former U.S. Postal Service employees pleaded guilty in federal court this week after admitting they stole cash and gift cards from the mail at an Atlanta facility. The thefts happened over a period of months before investigators caught on.

The case is a reminder of why your grandma's birthday card sometimes arrives empty. Federal prosecutors say the pair targeted letters and packages that looked like they might contain cash or gift cards. The scheme was pretty low-tech but effective enough to go undetected for a while.

Both defendants are awaiting sentencing. Postal inspectors say they take mail theft seriously and encourage anyone who suspects their mail has been tampered with to file a report.

stories.headline: Nine Georgia Chefs Named to 2026 Georgia Grown Executive Chef Class
stories.summary: The state just named nine chefs to its 2026 Georgia Grown Executive Chef class. The ceremony happened at the state capitol, celebrating chefs who champion locally sourced ingredients.
stories.body:
Nine chefs from across Georgia were inducted into the 2026 Georgia Grown Executive Chef class at a ceremony at the state capitol on March 11. The program highlights chefs who go out of their way to source ingredients from Georgia farms and producers.

The Georgia Grown program connects the state's agricultural producers directly with the restaurant industry, and the Executive Chef designation is basically a stamp of approval that these kitchens are putting Georgia ingredients front and center on their menus.

For diners, it's a useful signal. If you want to eat food that actually comes from Georgia rather than a Sysco truck, look for these chefs and their restaurants. The full list of 2026 honorees is on the Georgia Grown website.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia Grown Executive Chef ceremony at the state capitol

stories.headline: Alliance Theatre Reveals Kendeda Playwriting Prize Winner and Finalists
stories.summary: The Alliance Theatre announced the winner and finalists for their Kendeda Playwriting Prize. The winning play will get a full production at the Midtown theater.
stories.body:
The Alliance Theatre announced the winner of its Kendeda Playwriting Prize this week, along with the finalists who made the short list. The prize is one of the most prestigious new play awards in the country, and winning it means a full production at the Alliance's Midtown campus.

The Kendeda Prize has been a launching pad for plays that go on to major productions across the country. Previous winners have landed on Broadway and at regional theaters nationwide. For the Alliance, it's a way to position Atlanta as a city that develops new work rather than just importing it.

The winning play will premiere during the Alliance's upcoming season. Details on the production timeline and casting are expected in the coming weeks.

stories.headline: Tulsa Welding School Opens Atlanta Campus as Trade Jobs Boom
stories.summary: With roughly 400,000 skilled trade jobs sitting unfilled nationally, Tulsa Welding School just opened an Atlanta campus. The trades shortage is projected to hit 2 million by 2033.
stories.body:
Tulsa Welding School has opened an Atlanta campus, betting on the metro area's growing demand for skilled trades workers. The school says roughly 400,000 skilled trade positions are unfilled across the country right now, and that number could climb to nearly 2 million by 2033.

The Atlanta campus will offer welding and HVAC programs, targeting students who want to skip the four-year degree path and get into high-paying work faster. Starting salaries for certified welders in the Atlanta metro routinely top $50,000, with experienced welders earning significantly more.

The opening reflects a broader shift in how people think about career paths. With college costs continuing to climb and student debt weighing down a generation, trade schools are having a moment. Atlanta's construction boom and infrastructure needs make it an obvious market.
